name: Swarm uses Kademlia proximity order routing with 4+ redundant copies per neighborhood

links.targetTitle: Swarm stores all data as 4KB content-addressed chunks in a Merkle hash tree

global.title: Swarm uses Kademlia proximity order routing with 4+ redundant copies per neighborhood
global.content:
## Claim

Swarm's data layer is a **Kademlia overlay** where chunks are stored by the nodes whose 256-bit overlay addresses share the highest Proximity Order (PO) with the chunk address, with at least 4 replicas inside each responsible neighborhood.

## Evidence

- Each node has a 256-bit overlay address
- **Proximity Order (PO)**: the number of leading bits two addresses share
- Chunks are stored by the nodes with highest PO to the chunk address (the "responsible neighborhood")
- **Push-sync** moves chunks from the uploader to the responsible neighborhood
- **Pull-sync** replicates chunks within a neighborhood — ensures at least 4 copies
- **Cross-neighborhood**: erasure coding (Reed-Solomon) distributes parity chunks across neighborhoods
- **PSS-based recovery**: if a chunk is unavailable, a recovery protocol contacts known pinners who re-upload it

### Offline behavior

- Data uploaded to Swarm is held by the neighborhood, not just the uploading node — peers serve our data while we are offline
- When the local node comes back, pull-sync catches up on missed chunks
- **Stamps must still be valid** — expired stamps evict chunks from reserves to the cache, then LRU-evicted entirely

## Why it matters

Explains why the adapter's availability does not depend on our own Bee node staying online — as long as stamps are funded, the neighborhood replicates our data. It also frames why erasure coding is worth enabling for critical manifests.
global.description: Each node has a 256-bit overlay address; chunks stored by nodes with highest PO to the chunk address. Neighborhoods keep 4+ copies; erasure coding crosses neighborhoods.
